title: Junk journal ephemera collage spread
subtitle: Layering collected paper ephemera into a mixed-media journal spread
excerpt: A junk journal spread builds up from layers of collected paper: old envelopes, book pages, postage stamps, receipts, tissue paper, fabric scraps. The layers are glued in sequence from background to foreground, creating a dense, tactile page surface that functions as both a record and an artwork.
sourceNotes: Junk journalling as a named craft practice developed within the mixed-media and altered-books communities in the early 2000s. The collage techniques described here draw on the broader tradition of paper collage documented in art education literature and the scrapbooking craft community. No single attributable source.

content.text: A junk journal spread works by accumulation: each layer of
sits on the one below it, and the final surface is the sum of everything added. The

differs from a scrapbook in that the base pages themselves are often constructed from repurposed material, a folded brown envelope, a book page, a piece of wrapping paper, rather than clean white card. The constraints of found materials are part of the aesthetic.

items.qty: A loose pile sorted by general category
items.name: Collected ephemera: old envelopes, book pages, stamps, receipts, tissue paper, fabric scraps
PVA glue or gel medium, diluted 1:1 with water for background layers
Undiluted PVA or a glue stick for final-layer pieces
Craft knife and cutting mat for trimming
Bone folder for pressing pieces flat

content.text: Start with the largest and plainest pieces: strips of book text pages, pages of graph paper, sections of sheet music. Tear rather than cut these background pieces for

that soften the transitions between layers. Brush a coat of diluted PVA onto the journal page, lay the piece down, then brush another coat of diluted PVA over the top to seal it and bond it flat. Overlap pieces by 5 to 10 mm. The background layer does not need to look perfect; much of it will be covered.

Once the background layer is dry, add medium-scale pieces: old envelopes, labels, tissue paper, fabric scraps. These build texture and introduce the colour palette. Apply the same way: PVA under and over. Tissue paper applied over diluted PVA becomes almost transparent and blends the layers below; this is useful for softening a piece that reads too strongly as a separate element. Leave some of the background layer visible at the edges.
Foreground: feature pieces and writing space

The foreground layer holds the pieces you most want visible: a postage stamp, a photograph, a small illustration, a label. Apply these with undiluted PVA or a glue stick rather than the diluted version, so they sit sharply on top of the previous layers rather than sinking into them. Reserve a clear area on the spread for writing. A small envelope, left open and unglued on its flap, creates a pocket for folded notes or additional ephemera that can be removed and read.

attrs.intro: Problems in junk journal ephemera collage.

items.fix: Weight the open spread under a heavy board while still damp. For future spreads, dilute the PVA less (2:1 PVA to water rather than 1:1) and apply thinner coats, allowing each to dry before adding the next.
items.cause: Too much water in the diluted PVA is being absorbed by the base page, causing it to expand unevenly.
items.symptom: The page buckles and warps badly after layering

items.fix: Apply PVA under the piece only, not over it. Alternatively, brush a coat of matte medium over the finished dry spread to seal everything without further wetting individual pieces.
items.cause: The paper is too fragile for a wet-over coat of PVA.
items.symptom: Thin ephemera items (tissue, old receipts) tear when wet PVA is applied over them

attrs.heading: Common problems
